content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The next bus ___ (leave) at half past seven, so we have enough time for a coffee.
distractor_rationale: will is used for spontaneous decisions or predictions, not fixed public timetables
present continuous describes a personal arrangement, not a scheduled transport timetable
going to describes personal plans or evidence-based predictions, not fixed timetabled departures

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Look at those dark clouds — it ___ (rain) very soon.
distractor_rationale: will is for predictions without present evidence; the dark clouds are visible evidence that selects going to
present simple cannot express an imminent future event; it is used for timetables only
present continuous describes a fixed personal arrangement, not a weather prediction based on visual evidence

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The pharmacy ___ (close) at six o'clock every evening, including Saturdays.
distractor_rationale: will is for spontaneous decisions or open-ended predictions, not regular scheduled closing times
present continuous marks a specific personal arrangement on a particular day, not a regular timetabled routine
going to marks a personal intention or evidence-based prediction, not a fixed institutional schedule

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I ___ (meet) Anna at the new café on Friday — we booked a table there last week.
distractor_rationale: will is used for spontaneous decisions made at the moment of speaking, but the booking was made last week, so this is a fixed arrangement
present simple is reserved for official timetables such as trains or flights, not personal social arrangements
going to expresses intention but not a confirmed, booked arrangement; present continuous is the correct form for a fixed social appointment

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Don't worry about carrying all those bags — I ___ (help) you with them right now.
distractor_rationale: present continuous requires a prior arrangement; the decision to help is made spontaneously at this moment
going to implies a pre-formed intention decided before this moment, but the offer is clearly made on the spot
present simple cannot express a spontaneous future offer; it is used only for timetabled events

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We ___ (spend) our holiday in the mountains this summer — we decided back in January and already paid the deposit.
distractor_rationale: will is for spontaneous decisions or predictions, but this plan was made months ago and is a firm intention
present continuous signals a fixed confirmed arrangement such as a booking; here the focus is on the pre-formed decision and intention rather than a specific booked event
present simple cannot express a personal future plan; it is used only for official timetables

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The doctor ___ (see) you at three o'clock this afternoon — your appointment is already confirmed in the system.
distractor_rationale: present simple is for publicly scheduled timetables like trains or classes, not individual confirmed appointments described from the doctor's perspective as a future certainty
going to describes a personal intention; here the sentence presents a confirmed future fact about the appointment from an institutional perspective, making will appropriate

present continuous is correct when describing the patient's own fixed arrangement, but this sentence presents the event from a third-party scheduling perspective where will conveys the confirmed future fact

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom has eaten almost nothing all day and he ___ (faint) if he doesn't have something to eat very soon.
distractor_rationale: will is for predictions based on general belief or opinion without present visible evidence; the observable evidence here — Tom eating almost nothing — demands going to
present simple cannot express an imminent future event; it describes only timetabled or habitual actions
present continuous describes a fixed personal arrangement; fainting is not an arrangement but an imminent event predicted from current evidence

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The conference ___ (start) at nine sharp tomorrow, so all speakers must be in the hall at least fifteen minutes before.
distractor_rationale: will is inappropriate for a formally scheduled institutional event with a fixed time on an official programme; present simple is required for timetabled events
present continuous marks a personal social arrangement; a conference on an official programme is a timetabled event requiring present simple
going to marks personal intention or evidence-based prediction; an institutionally scheduled conference on a fixed programme requires present simple

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Sara ___ (fly) to the regional office next Tuesday — her manager confirmed the trip and booked her ticket yesterday.
distractor_rationale: will is for spontaneous decisions made at the moment of speaking; Sara's trip was confirmed and booked in advance, making it a fixed arrangement
going to expresses a pre-formed intention or decision; because the ticket is already booked and the arrangement is fully confirmed, present continuous is the more precise choice
present simple is reserved for publicly scheduled timetables such as transport or class schedules, not individual travel arrangements

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The last bus ___ (leave) the station at 11:30 tonight, so don't be late.
distractor_rationale: will is used for spontaneous decisions or predictions, not fixed timetable departures
present continuous describes a personal arrangement, not a scheduled public transport timetable
going to describes personal plans or evidence-based predictions, not official scheduled services

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We ___ (meet) Anna and her colleagues at the conference centre at 9am tomorrow — she booked the room last week.
distractor_rationale: will is for spontaneous decisions made at the moment of speaking, but this arrangement was made in advance
present simple is for official timetables such as trains or flights, not personal social arrangements
going to describes a general intention or plan, but present continuous is more appropriate when a specific time and place have been fixed in advance

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I haven't decided on a gift yet, but I think I ___ (buy) her some flowers from the shop on the corner.
distractor_rationale: present continuous requires a fixed prior arrangement, but the speaker has just admitted they haven't decided yet
going to requires a formed intention decided before the moment of speaking, but the speaker is deciding right now
present simple is for scheduled timetable events, not spontaneous decisions made during conversation

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom has already saved enough money and found a flat he likes, so he ___ (move) out of his parents' house next month.
distractor_rationale: will is for spontaneous decisions or open predictions without prior evidence; here the completed preparations — saving money and finding a flat — provide concrete evidence of a formed intention

present continuous is used when a specific date, time, and arrangement with another party have been confirmed; moving out of one's own home does not require external scheduling, so going to is more appropriate than present continuous here
present simple is restricted to officially published timetables such as transport schedules, not personal life decisions

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The museum ___ at nine o'clock every morning, including Sundays.
distractor_rationale: will open is used for spontaneous decisions or predictions, not for fixed institutional timetables
is opening implies a personal arrangement, not a scheduled timetable event
is going to open implies a plan or evidence-based prediction, not a regular schedule

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Look at those black clouds — it ___ very soon.
distractor_rationale: will rain is for predictions without visible evidence; here the black clouds provide clear physical evidence, requiring going to
rains is present simple used for timetables, not evidence-based predictions
is raining describes an action happening right now, not a near-future prediction based on evidence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The last bus ___ at midnight, so we need to leave the party before then.
distractor_rationale: will leave suggests a spontaneous or predicted event, not a fixed transport timetable
is leaving suggests a personal arrangement, not a scheduled timetable departure
is going to leave implies intention or evidence-based prediction, not a fixed schedule

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna has already bought her ticket — she ___ the concert next Saturday.
distractor_rationale: will attend is used for spontaneous decisions made at the moment of speaking, but the ticket purchase shows this is a pre-arranged plan
attends is present simple for timetables, not for personal social arrangements
is going to attend could express intention, but the purchased ticket makes this a confirmed fixed arrangement, best expressed with present continuous

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: I haven't decided where to go on holiday yet, but I think I ___ somewhere warm this summer.
distractor_rationale: am going implies a fixed arrangement already made, but the sentence says no decision has been taken yet
go is present simple, used for timetables, not personal predictions without a plan
am going to go implies a formed intention or plan, but the speaker explicitly says they haven't decided yet

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We have reserved a table at the new restaurant — we ___ there with Tom and Sara on Friday evening.
distractor_rationale: will eat is for spontaneous decisions or predictions, not confirmed reservations already made
eat is present simple for scheduled timetables, not personal social arrangements
are going to eat expresses intention, but a confirmed reservation makes this a fixed arrangement, best expressed by present continuous

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Mark has decided to change jobs — he ___ his own small business next year.
distractor_rationale: will start is for spontaneous decisions made at the moment of speaking, but the sentence states the decision has already been made
starts is present simple for fixed timetables, not personal future plans
is starting implies a confirmed arrangement with a specific time fixed, but the sentence describes a formed intention rather than a booked event

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The phone is ringing but my hands are full of shopping bags — don't worry, I ___ it.
distractor_rationale: am going to answer implies a pre-formed intention, but the decision to answer is being made spontaneously at the moment of speaking
am answering describes an action in progress right now, not a decision just made in response to the situation
answer is present simple for timetables or habitual actions, not for a spontaneous offer made in the moment

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: According to the programme, the opening ceremony ___ at exactly half past seven tomorrow evening.
distractor_rationale: will start implies a prediction or spontaneous decision, not an event fixed in an official published programme
is starting implies a personal arrangement, but an official ceremony listed in a programme is a timetabled event
is going to start implies intention or evidence-based prediction, but a programme entry represents a fixed institutional schedule

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Lisa has been saving money for months and has already spoken to an estate agent — she ___ a flat in the city centre before the end of the year.
distractor_rationale: will buy is for spontaneous decisions or general predictions without prior evidence, but months of saving and speaking to an agent show a clear prior intention
is buying suggests a specific confirmed arrangement with a date fixed, whereas the sentence describes a strong intention not yet finalised as a booking
buys is present simple for habitual or timetabled events, not for a personal future plan based on evidence of prior preparation

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Look at those dark clouds — it will rain very soon.
content.error_sentence: Look at those dark clouds — it will rain very soon.
content.corrected_sentence: Look at those dark clouds — it is going to rain very soon.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The train to the city centre will leave at 8:15 every morning.
content.error_sentence: The train to the city centre will leave at 8:15 every morning.
content.corrected_sentence: The train to the city centre leaves at 8:15 every morning.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria will meet her dentist tomorrow afternoon — she booked the appointment two weeks ago.
content.error_sentence: Maria will meet her dentist tomorrow afternoon — she booked the appointment two weeks ago.
content.corrected_sentence: Maria is meeting her dentist tomorrow afternoon — she booked the appointment two weeks ago.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom is going to help you carry those bags — he just offered when he saw you struggling.
content.error_sentence: Tom is going to help you carry those bags — he just offered when he saw you struggling.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom will help you carry those bags — he just offered when he saw you struggling.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: We will fly to the coast next Friday — we bought the tickets online last month.
content.error_sentence: We will fly to the coast next Friday — we bought the tickets online last month.
content.corrected_sentence: We are flying to the coast next Friday — we bought the tickets online last month.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: According to the school timetable, the autumn term will start on the third of September this year.
content.error_sentence: According to the school timetable, the autumn term will start on the third of September this year.
content.corrected_sentence: According to the school timetable, the autumn term starts on the third of September this year.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: James is having a headache, so he is staying home tonight instead of going to the party.
content.error_sentence: James is having a headache, so he is staying home tonight instead of going to the party.
content.corrected_sentence: James has a headache, so he is staying home tonight instead of going to the party.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If you look at her training schedule, you can see that Sara is going to run a marathon next spring — she has been preparing for months.
content.error_sentence: If you look at her training schedule, you can see that Sara is going to run a marathon next spring — she has been preparing for months.
content.corrected_sentence: If you look at her training schedule, you can see that Sara is running a marathon next spring — she has been preparing for months.

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Look at those dark clouds — it ___ (rain) very soon.
distractor_rationale: will is for predictions without present visual evidence; here the dark clouds provide clear evidence
present simple is for fixed timetable events, not weather predictions based on visible signs
present continuous describes a fixed personal arrangement, not a prediction based on current evidence

duplicate: exact duplicate of a prior item
